title: Cat Abdominal Heaving: Is It a Veterinary Emergency?
meta_desc: If your cat is using their stomach to breathe, it is a high-priority medical emergency. Learn what abdominal heaving means and what to do next.
content_html:
TL;DR: Abdominal heaving is a life-threatening emergency in cats indicating severe respiratory distress; if you see your cat's belly pumping or they are open-mouth breathing, seek immediate veterinary care.

What does it mean if my cat is experiencing abdominal heaving?

Abdominal heaving, often referred to as labored breathing or dyspnea, occurs when a cat uses its abdominal muscles to assist in moving air into and out of the lungs. Normally, a cat's breathing is quiet and involves subtle chest movement. When you see the belly 'pumping' or moving forcefully with every breath, it is a sign that the cat is struggling to maintain adequate oxygen levels.

Should I treat my cat's abdominal heaving as a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for abdominal heaving is High, as cats are evolutionarily designed to hide illness until they are in crisis.
  • Visible signs of respiratory distress typically indicate that the animal is in a state of life-threatening emergency requiring immediate intervention.
  • If your cat is panting (open-mouth breathing) or has a blue or purple tint to their gums, they require immediate oxygen therapy at an emergency clinic.

How can a photo or video of my cat’s breathing help the veterinary team with triage?

  • If it is safe to do so without delaying your departure, record a 10-second video of your cat’s breathing at rest.
  • Stress during transport can alter a cat's breathing rate or mask symptoms; a video allows the triage team to see the true severity of the heaving.
  • Providing visual evidence helps the triage nurse quickly categorize the situation, which may lead to your cat receiving priority access to life-saving oxygen support.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Persistent vomiting, depression, abdominal discomfort, and/or fever warrant careful monitoring and reevaluation, including abdominal palpation to detect foreign bodies or abdominal discomfort. In cats presenting with possible gastrointestinal obstruction secondary to a foreign body, clinical signs often include vomiting and anorexia, although vomiting may be less common with distal small-intestinal obstruction. Physical examination should be thorough, including inspection of the oral cavity for linear foreign bodies anchored to the base of the tongue; these should be cut immediately, never pulled. Signs of hypovolemic shock and abdominal pain usually accompany intestinal incarceration, and laboratory findings may reveal leukocytosis with a left shift, or marked leukocytosis or leukopenia with a degenerative left shift in cases of gastrointestinal perforation and secondary bacterial peritonitis.

Chapter: Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 390)

faqs.question: Should I wait for my regular vet to open?
faqs.answer: No. Abdominal heaving is a critical emergency. If your regular veterinarian is not available immediately, proceed to the nearest 24-hour emergency animal hospital.

faqs.question: What are common causes of labored breathing in cats?
faqs.answer: Common causes include feline asthma, congestive heart failure (fluid buildup), pleural effusion, or severe pneumonia.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human asthma medication?
faqs.answer: Never give your cat human medications. Many are toxic to cats, and without a proper diagnosis, you could significantly worsen their condition.
